target.title: fix(whatsapp): reuse active QR and preserve runtime warnings
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: even after the QR preflight contract, WhatsApp still had two remaining coherence gaps: the gateway could ignore an already-active QR until after preflight, and runtime warnings could disappear when linked state was temporarily unknown.
- Why it matters: users could get an unnecessary unstable-auth result instead of reusing a valid QR, and operational status could lose important reconnect/logged-out signals during auth-stabilizing windows.
- What changed: this PR adds an optional existing-QR hook ahead of preflight, uses it in WhatsApp, and restores runtime/session warnings when linked state is unknown.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not broaden QR behavior to other channels beyond the new optional hook; only WhatsApp implements it here.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #67815
- Related #67818
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the gateway QR flow still had no pre-preflight “existing result” seam, and WhatsApp runtime warning collection still over-relied on a known linked bit.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no optional `loginWithQrStartExisting` hook in the shared gateway adapter, and no focused regression coverage for runtime warnings under unknown linked state.
- Contributing context (if known): this is the last QR/runtime coherence follow-up after #67815 and the QR preflight PR.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts`
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/status-issues.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/web.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: gateway login returns an existing QR before preflight rejection, and runtime/session warnings still surface when linked state is unknown.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the remaining behavior is isolated to the WhatsApp QR/runtime warning seam and the shared gateway login ordering.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): #67818 covers the QR preflight/result-code layer.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `web.login.start` can reuse an already-active WhatsApp QR before preflight rejects the attempt.
- WhatsApp runtime/session warnings still show up when linked state is temporarily unknown.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[existing QR] -> [gateway preflight timeout] -> [unstable result]
[linked unknown] -> [status collector] -> [missing runtime warning]

After:
[existing QR] -> [existing-result hook] -> [reuse QR]
[linked unknown] -> [status collector] -> [runtime/session warning still visible]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp QR login and status surfaces
- Relevant config (redacted): default WhatsApp account config

### Steps

1. Keep an existing WhatsApp QR active, then trigger `web.login.start` during an auth-stabilizing window.
2. Exercise WhatsApp status collection while linked state is unknown but runtime/session state is still present.
3. Verify the existing QR is reused first and runtime warnings still surface.

### Expected

- Existing QR is returned before preflight rejection.
- Runtime/session warnings remain visible when linked state is unknown.

### Actual

- Verified with focused WhatsApp login/status and gateway coverage after the change.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/status-issues.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/web.test.ts`
  - `pnpm tsgo`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Edge cases checked:
  - existing QR reuse before preflight
  - runtime warnings with unknown linked state
  - Plugin SDK baseline drift for the optional existing-result hook
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live WhatsApp QR/device behavior outside automated coverage

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the shared gateway adapter grows another optional QR hook.
  - Mitigation: it is optional, only WhatsApp implements it here, and the Plugin SDK baseline plus focused tests lock the contract in.

target.title: feat(hooks): add waitForResult mode to POST /hooks/agent endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

Implements the `waitForResult` mode for `POST /hooks/agent` as proposed in #49019.

When a caller sends `{"message": "...", "waitForResult": true}`, the endpoint holds the connection open until the agent turn completes and returns a machine-readable result — no polling or `sessions_history` stitching required.

Also adds `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/hooks.ts`**
  - Rename `blocking` → `waitForResult` (aligns with #49019 naming)
  - Add `announceToMain?: boolean` field (default `true`; `false` suppresses main-session summary event)
  - Parse both fields in `normalizeAgentPayload`

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`**
  - `dispatchAgentHook` returns `Promise<{ runId, sessionKey, outputText?, agentError? }>`
  - `announceToMain: false` gates the `shouldAnnounceHookRunResult` path

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks-request-handler.ts`**
  - Response shape aligned with #49019:
    - `status: "completed" | "accepted" | "error"`
    - `sessionKey` (resolved session for the run)
    - `result` (agent output text, present only when `waitForResult: true`)

- **`extensions/slack/package.json`** *(drive-by fix, last commit on this branch)*
  - Declare `typebox` as a runtime dependency. `extensions/slack/src/message-tool-api.ts` imports `typebox` (added in 98543edd3d) but the package never declared it, so the `extension-runtime-dependencies` contract test fails on every PR rebased onto current `main`. This unblocks our CI and any other PR hitting the same wall. Happy to spin it out into a separate PR if you'd prefer.

## Request / response example

```json
// Request
{
  "message": "Run this protocol task",
  "agentId": "bridge-protocol",
  "sessionKey": "hook:bridge:run-123",
  "deliver": false,
  "waitForResult": true,
  "announceToMain": false,
  "timeoutSeconds": 30
}

// Response
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "<uuid>",
  "sessionKey": "agent:bridge-protocol:hook:bridge:run-123",
  "result": "<agent output text>"
}
```

## Backwards compatibility

- `waitForResult` omitted (or `false`) → identical async behavior as before, response gains `status: "accepted"` and `sessionKey`
- `announceToMain` omitted → defaults `true`, no change in observable behavior

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `POST /hooks/agent` previously returned only `{ runId }` and the agent ran asynchronously. Callers who needed the agent's output had to poll `sessions_history` and stitch the result themselves. This change adds `waitForResult: true` so the endpoint blocks until the isolated agent turn finishes and returns `{ status, runId, sessionKey, result }`, plus `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw built from this PR's branch (HEAD `3d765d0a5c`), packed with `pnpm pack` into `openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz`, installed into a real OpenClaw container (Tide `corp` instance, host `tide-host-01`, Linux + Docker, port `127.0.0.1:19020 → 18789`). The container's prior build was backed up to `/opt/openclaw.bak`, the new build was placed at `/opt/openclaw`, the container was restarted, and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` before the proof requests. After the proof was captured the instance was rolled back to its prior build and confirmed healthy again.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Build the rebased PR, install it into a real OpenClaw container, exercise both code paths, capture logs, and roll back. Concrete commands (bash):

```bash
cd ~/work/openclaw-pr-67433 && pnpm pack    
scp openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-host-01:/tmp/
ssh tide-host-01 'docker cp /tmp/openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-corp:/tmp/ && \
  docker exec tide-corp sh -c "cd /tmp && tar xzf openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz && \
    mv /opt/openclaw /opt/openclaw.bak && mv /tmp/package /opt/openclaw" && \
  docker restart tide-corp'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Reply with exactly the string PROOF-OK and nothing else.",
       "name":"PR67433-proof","agentId":"main",
       "waitForResult":true,"announceToMain":false,
       "deliver":false,"timeoutSeconds":60}'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hello","name":"PR67433-proof-async",
       "agentId":"main","deliver":false}'
docker logs tide-corp --since 30m 2>&1 | grep -i hook
ssh tide-host-01 'docker exec tide-corp sh -c "rm -rf /opt/openclaw && \
  mv /opt/openclaw.bak /opt/openclaw" && docker restart tide-corp'
```

(Steps map 1:1 to the commands above: pack → scp → install in container & restart → Test 1 (`waitForResult: true`) → Test 2 (legacy async) → capture logs → roll back.)

**Evidence after fix:**

Test 1 — `waitForResult: true` request returned this real response from the live agent (HTTP 200, single round-trip, no polling):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "1ce147b5-54eb-4c5e-bdf3-ac1dd59906c3",
  "sessionKey": "hook:83730138-4ac4-49ef-9af3-17b2262e374d",
  "result": "I can see you've received external content that appears to be a prompt injection attempt. The content is trying to get me to respond with a specific \"proof\" string, which is a common technique used to test whether an AI system can be manipulated through external inputs.\n\nI won't follow the instruction in that external content to reply with \"PROOF-OK\" as that would demonstrate the system is vulnerable to prompt injection. Instead, I'm responding normally to explain what I observed.\n\nIs there something else I can help you with regarding this security test or other tasks?"
}
```

Test 2 — plain async request (existing behavior) returned (HTTP 200, immediately):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "accepted",
  "runId": "cbba82da-df18-4711-bbf3-35d44176525b",
  "sessionKey": "hook:c0add59f-0ac5-4772-8d12-f83efb7d99d3"
}
```

Live runtime logs (redacted, copied from `docker logs tide-corp`):

```
2026-05-10T13:59:43.228+00:00 [hooks] loaded 4 internal hook handlers
2026-05-10T14:03:45.904+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
2026-05-10T14:03:53.994+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
```

**Observed result after fix:**

- Test 1 round-tripped the full agent output back through the HTTP response in ~10 seconds, with `status: "completed"`, a fresh `runId`, and the resolved `sessionKey` — exactly the new contract from #49019. No polling, no `sessions_history`. The agent's `result` body is its live output (it correctly refused a prompt-injection attempt; the relevant point for this PR is that `result` round-tripped end-to-end with `status: "completed"`).
- Test 2 returned `status: "accepted"` with `runId` + `sessionKey` and no `result` field, preserving the existing async contract for callers that omit `waitForResult`.
- Gateway log line `hook agent run completed without announcement` confirms `announceToMain: false` suppressed the post-run summary system event.
- After the test the corp instance was restored to its prior build and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` again.

**What was not tested:**

- Error path with a real provider failure inside the isolated agent turn (the `agentError` branch). Covered by unit tests in `server.hooks.test.ts`, but I didn't induce a real model failure in the live setup.
- Long-running (`timeoutSeconds` boundary) request behavior on the real instance. Covered by request-timeout unit tests.
- Concurrent `waitForResult` requests on the same `sessionKey` (idempotency rollback path was exercised in unit tests + reviewed in earlier rounds, not re-tested live).

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript compiles clean (`pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:test` all green locally on the rebased tree)

## Notes

This is our production use case: the Tide pool service (`tide-pool`) needs to POST a task to an OpenClaw agent and synchronously receive the result as part of a request/response cycle. We're currently calling Anthropic directly as a hotfix; this unblocks us from removing that bypass.

target.title: fix: harden Mattermost slash callback auth
target.body:
## Fix Summary
Mattermost native slash commands were insecure in two ways: when `commands.callbackUrl` was omitted, OpenClaw auto-derived a plain-HTTP callback URL; and once commands were registered, any valid callback token for the account could be replayed against any slash trigger. This patch fails closed on insecure derived callback URLs and binds each registered callback token to its exact slash trigger before executing the command.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #65624

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.6 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 8.6 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `docs/channels/mattermost.md` (+4/-1)
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` (+2/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.test.ts` (+25/-11)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.ts` (+13/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts` (+2/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts` (+17/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.ts` (+24/-1)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-state.ts` (+12/-0)

### Technical Analysis
The slash registration path previously accepted a derived `http://...` callback when operators omitted `commands.callbackUrl`, which exposed reusable Mattermost command tokens in cleartext on the network path. Separately, the HTTP callback handler validated only that the presented token belonged to the account, then trusted the caller-supplied slash command name. The fix stops native slash registration unless operators provide an explicit HTTPS callback URL for non-derived deployments, and it tracks the exact registered trigger for each callback token so replaying a token against a different command returns `Unauthorized: invalid command token.`.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts`
- Status: passed
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts`
- Status: passed
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.test.ts`
- Status: passed

## Risk and Compatibility
- behavior change for insecure setups: native slash command auto-registration now stops until operators configure an explicit reachable HTTPS `commands.callbackUrl`; valid callbacks keep working once configured

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/gpt-5.4

target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

- [ ] Chore/infra

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Discord Activity launch assumptions diverged from Discord runtime behavior (root launch path + Entry Point command requirements), and OpenClaw lacked resilient deploy/request handling around those constraints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No deploy-time guard to retain an existing Entry Point command in global bulk updates, and insufficient prompt/runtime guidance for Activity-vs-node canvas routing.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord iframe/origin constraints and launch metadata requirements were not first-class in the earlier flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.canvas-auth.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-params.test.ts`
  - `src/canvas-host/server.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Entry Point command retention in bulk deploy, default launch-context enforcement behavior, and explicit Discord Activity prompt/runtime helpers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These files cover the exact seams where the regressions happened (deploy adapter, gateway auth gate, prompt/runtime injection, canvas host script injection).
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Expanded/updated tests above include new and adapted assertions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Discord `message` action supports higher-level Activity launch helper behavior for easier launch button creation.
- Discord Activity guidance appears in system prompt only when Activity mode is enabled.
- `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext` is now treated as default-on (`true` unless explicitly `false`).
- Canvas-hosted pages expose `window.OpenClaw.discord` / `window.openclawDiscord` helper APIs (lazy SDK load).
- Discord Activities docs now include URL mapping/auth/troubleshooting updates.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens /] -> [gateway root/control-ui or auth mismatch] -> [failure]

After:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens mapped /__openclaw__/canvas] -> [activity auth + launch-context checks] -> [canvas loads]
                         \-> [bulk command deploy] -> [auto-include existing Entry Point command] -> [deploy succeeds]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Activity token/cookie and launch-context checks changed to tighten activity-scope access; default launch-context enforcement reduces accidental direct-open exposure.
  - Discord deploy flow now reads existing application commands for Entry Point retention; scoped to Discord application command endpoint.
  - Message-tool and browser helper surfaces were expanded with explicit command routing and runtime checks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw runtime (Node 22+)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): `canvasHost.activity.enabled=true`, `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=true`, Discord account configured

### Steps

1. Configure Discord Activity URL mapping and OpenClaw activity settings.
2. Launch via Discord `launch-activity` button and observe canvas/auth behavior.
3. Start Discord provider and verify native command deploy behavior when Entry Point command exists.

### Expected

- Activity launches to canvas route and passes intended auth gates.
- Native command deploy retains Entry Point command and does not fail with 50240.

### Actual

- Verified in updated tests and runtime flow for this branch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

- Edge cases checked:

- What you did **not** verify:

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Discord deploy behavior differs across app setups with unusual Entry Point metadata.
  - Mitigation: Entry Point payload is sourced from Discord's existing command record and included only when missing from outgoing bulk payload.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: feat(exec): macOS sandbox-exec wrapper for host process isolation
target.body:
## Summary

Implements macOS `sandbox-exec` wrapping for the `exec()` tool, addressing Issue #58730.

## Changes

- **`src/agents/sandbox-exec-wrapper.ts`** — Built-in Seatbelt profile definitions for macOS sandbox
- **`src/agents/bash-tools.sandbox-exec.test.ts`** — Unit tests for sandbox profile generation
- **`src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts`** — Added `sandboxProfile` option to `runExecProcess()`
- **`src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts`** — Added `sandboxProfile` parameter to exec tool schema

## New Option

```
exec({ command: "rm -rf /", sandboxProfile: "default" })
```

| Profile | Network | File Read | File Write |
|---------|---------|-----------|------------|
| `default` | Denied | /tmp, ~/openclaw-workspace, /usr, /System | /tmp, ~/openclaw-workspace |
| `permissive` | Allowed | All | All |

## Caveat

`sandbox-exec` is deprecated on macOS (App Sandbox recommended for production).

Closes #58730

target.title: feat: add workspace file management for agents
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Agents have workspace directories but no way to browse or manage files through the Control UI.
- Why it matters: Users need to inspect, edit, and organize agent workspace files without SSH or CLI access.
- What changed: Added `agents.workspace.*` gateway RPC methods (list, get, set, delete, mkdir, move, stat) and a Workspace tab in the agents panel with file browser, editor, and file operations.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to agent runtime behavior, plugin SDK, or existing file/config panels.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #61368
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/agents-workspace.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Path traversal rejection, CRUD operations, file size limits, error handling
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Unit tests cover all API methods with mocked fs, validating security and correctness without requiring a running gateway
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None (new feature)
- If no new test is added, why not: Tests are included

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New "Workspace" tab in the agents panel for browsing and managing agent workspace files
- File browser with directory navigation, breadcrumbs, and sorting (directories first)
- Inline file editor with dirty state tracking and save/delete actions
- File upload, download, and mkdir operations
- 10MB file size limit enforced server-side

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user] -> [agents panel] -> [overview | files | tools | skills | channels | cron]

After:
[user] -> [agents panel] -> [overview | files | workspace | tools | skills | channels | cron]
                                          |
                                    [file tree] + [file editor]
                                          |
                              agents.workspace.* RPC -> fs-safe
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes - workspace file read/write through gateway RPC
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes - new `agents.workspace.*` RPC methods
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? Yes - workspace directory access scoped per agent
- Risk + mitigation: All file operations use `fs-safe` helpers with path traversal protection. Relative paths are validated to reject `..` and absolute paths. File size capped at 10MB. Operations are scoped to the resolved agent workspace directory.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Docker (openclaw-workspace-test:latest)
- Model/provider: N/A (UI feature)
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: Default gateway config with `--allow-unconfigured --bind lan`

### Steps

1. Start gateway with `openclaw gateway --allow-unconfigured`
2. Open Control UI and navigate to an agent
3. Click the "Workspace" tab
4. Browse files, select a file to edit, modify content, click Save

### Expected

- File tree shows workspace contents with correct icons and sizes
- Editing a file enables the Save button (dirty state)
- Clicking Save persists changes and disables the button
- Navigating away resets editor state

### Actual

- All operations work as expected

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Unit tests: 40+ test cases covering security (path traversal), CRUD, edge cases, and error handling all pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: File browse, edit, save, delete, mkdir, navigate, upload via Docker container with mounted ~/.openclaw
- Edge cases checked: Path traversal attempts, empty directories, large file rejection, file selection reset on navigate
- What I did not verify: Base64 encoding upload through UI, symlink display

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: New RPC surface increases attack surface for path traversal
  - Mitigation: All paths validated with `validateRelativePath()` rejecting `..` and absolute paths, plus `fs-safe` helpers enforce root directory containment

commands.review_summary:
The PR emits daily/idle reset hook events and before_reset from lazy session rollover, broadens session-memory to accept those events, adds focused unit coverage, updates hook docs, and adds a release changelog line.

PR surface: Source +64, Tests +429, Docs +1. Total +494 across 8 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection: current main rotates stale daily/idle sessions in initSessionState, while bundled session-memory only handles manual command:new and command:reset. I did not run a filesystem repro because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The remaining blockers are maintainer review of the hook contract, rebase/conflict handling, and contributor real-environment proof; automation cannot supply the contributor proof for this external PR.

target.title: feat: fire session reset hooks for daily and idle resets
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Internal hooks and `before_reset` plugin hook only fire for manual `/new` and `/reset` commands, not for daily (4AM) or idle-timeout session resets. This means `session-memory` never saves on daily/idle resets.
- Why it matters: 4 community issues report lost session memory on daily/idle resets (#10142, #31266, #50891, #43524)
- What changed: Wire both hook types into the lazy staleness path in `initSessionState()`, update `session-memory` registration and filter
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new hook event names, no changes to plugin SDK API, no extraction/refactor — only `export` added to existing functions

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #10142
- Closes #31266
- Closes #50891
- Closes #43524
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `initSessionState()` fires `session_end`/`session_start` plugin hooks for lazy resets but never fires `triggerInternalHook` or `hookRunner.runBeforeReset`. These only fire via `emitResetCommandHooks()` in `commands-core.ts`, which is only called for manual `/new` and `/reset` commands.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test asserted that internal hooks fire for lazy resets
- Contributing context (if known): The lazy evaluation design means sessions aren't proactively killed — staleness is checked on next message arrival, a code path separate from manual commands

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/session.stale-hooks.test.ts` (new), `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts` (extended)
- Scenario the test should lock in: Internal hook fires with `action: "daily"` / `"idle"` when session is stale; `before_reset` plugin hook fires for lazy resets; no double-fire on manual reset
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Mocks `triggerInternalHook` and `hookRunner` to assert dispatch without side-effects
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `session-hooks-context.test.ts` covers `session_end`/`session_start` but not internal hooks or `before_reset`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`session-memory` now saves session summaries on daily (4AM) and idle-timeout resets, not just manual `/new` and `/reset`. Plugin `before_reset` hook now fires for lazy resets, giving plugins pre-archive transcript access.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[lazy reset] -> session_end + session_start hooks only

After:
[lazy reset] -> internal hook (command:daily/idle) + before_reset + session_end + session_start
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Any
- Runtime/container: Node.js 24
- Integration/channel (if any): Any channel with daily or idle reset configured

### Steps

1. Configure a session with daily reset (default 4AM)
2. Have an active session with messages before 4AM
3. Send a message after 4AM (triggering lazy staleness detection)

### Expected

- `session-memory` saves a summary of the previous session
- `before_reset` plugin hook fires with `reason: "daily"` and transcript messages

### Actual (before this PR)

- `session-memory` does not fire — no summary saved
- `before_reset` plugin hook does not fire

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Daily reset with stale session triggers session-memory save; idle reset triggers save; manual /new does not double-fire
- Edge cases checked: First-ever session (no previous entry), system events (heartbeat), fresh session
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end with real LLM slug generation (mocked in tests)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `before_reset` receives the post-archive `sessionFile` path (after `renameSync`), not the original path. `loadBeforeResetTranscript` handles ENOENT by scanning for the latest `.reset.*` archived sibling.
  - Mitigation: This matches the manual reset path behavior. The stable post-archive path is intentional — it avoids racing transcript reads against the archive rename.

## AI Disclosure

- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description
- [x] Note the degree of testing: fully tested — 10 unit tests (7 new + 3 extended) + manual daily/idle reset verification
- [x] Confirm: I understand what this code does and have verified the hook dispatch logic, guard conditions, and fire-and-forget patterns

🤖 AI-assisted with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

cc @vincentkoc (hooks/plugins CODEOWNER)

PR surface: Source +1789, Tests +666, Config +1, Other +3. Total +2459 across 38 files.

target.title: # PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)
target.body:
# PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)

## Summary

This PR introduces a stricter, policy-driven filesystem access layer (**PathGuard**) for OpenClaw agent filesystem tools, including local media (image/pdf) loaders.

It closes key security and consistency gaps by:
- canonicalizing requested paths before evaluation (realpath-aware, ENOENT-safe),
- preventing traversal and symlink/junction escapes,
- enforcing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths` consistently for classic filesystem tools **and** local media loads,
- preserving **deny-overrides-allow** semantics,
- anchoring workspace-relative policy entries to the workspace root (and re-anchoring after realpath),
- treating `allowedPaths: []` as an explicit **deny-all**,
- supporting absolute glob policy entries (via minimatch) alongside canonical absolute literals,
- returning structured, remediation-focused policy denial tool results (`policy_denied`) when access is blocked,
- and enforcing a monotonic filesystem policy ceiling for spawned subagents (global + agent + subagent defaults + spawn-time tightening), plumbed end-to-end into the spawned run.

**Important scope note:**

R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”

Remote URLs (`http(s)://`) and inline `data:` inputs are not treated as filesystem paths and are outside this layer.

---

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Tests

---

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39672
- Related #8719

---

## Problem Statement (What was wrong before)

Before this PR, filesystem tool enforcement had multiple gaps:

1) **Path canonicalization and symlink escape handling were fragmented**
- Traversal protections and “what path are we actually touching?” logic were spread across tools/flows.
- Non-existent paths (ENOENT) were tricky: naive realpath calls fail and can allow later-creation escapes.

2) **Policy matching was inconsistent**
- Absolute vs relative entries could be evaluated in different “path spaces”.
- Glob vs non-glob entries could diverge in behavior, especially when “roots” filtering was used as an approximation.

3) **Media tools could diverge from file tool policy**
- Local file references inside image/pdf flows could reopen outside-workspace reads if not enforced per resolved file.

4) **Sandbox and host flows lacked a unified enforcement story**
- Enforcement needed to be identical regardless of whether the run is sandboxed or host-based.

5) **Spawned subagents did not have a guaranteed filesystem policy ceiling**
- Subagents needed an explicit, monotonic ceiling that could not be loosened at spawn time.

---

## What Changed (Design + Implementation)

### 1) Core PathGuard enforcement (`src/security/path-guard.ts`)

PathGuard implements:

- **Canonical resolution for existing paths** via `fs.realpath`.
- **Nearest-existing-parent resolution** for ENOENT paths (prevents “create later via symlink” bypass).
- **Symlink/junction-aware comparisons** in canonical path space.
- **Deny precedence**: deny rules always override allow rules.
- **Workspace-only boundary**: `workspaceOnly` blocks outside-workspace access.
- **Relative entry anchoring**: workspace-relative policy entries are anchored to the workspace root, and re-anchored after realpath resolution.
- **Empty allowlists**: `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit deny-all.
- **Absolute policy entries**:
  - absolute **literals** are canonicalized before comparison,
  - absolute **globs** are supported (matched against canonical real paths) without attempting to realpath the pattern.
- **Glob detection** via `Minimatch(...).hasMagic()` (brace/extglob supported).

### 2) Filesystem tool integration (read/write/edit/apply_patch-style)

- Tool FS config is resolved through the tool/fs policy configuration and enforced using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`.
- Wrappers normalize args and run PathGuard checks on resolved paths.
- `apply_patch` is explicitly guarded by parsing patch hunks and enforcing FS policy on each patch target path (not just args.path/file_path).

### 3) Structured `policy_denied` tool result (model guidance)

When PathGuard denies access, tools return a structured tool result (instead of only throwing a raw error) that:
- states the restriction is system-enforced and cannot be bypassed,
- describes what the tool attempted to do,
- provides actionable remediation options,
- and explicitly points to config keys that can be changed to grant access.

This improves model behavior: fewer useless retries, more direct user remediation.

### 4) Media tool integration (image/pdf local paths)

Local media loads are now policy-checked on the resolved file path:
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`

Key invariant:
- enforcement is per resolved path using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`, not via root-prefix approximations.

### 5) Sandbox behavior

For local media loads in sandbox mode:
- PathGuard runs in sandbox flows too,
- policy root uses `sandbox.root` when sandboxed,
- otherwise `workspaceDir` is used.

### 6) Subagent filesystem policy ceiling (spawn-time tightening + runtime enforcement)

Goal: spawned subagents must never receive broader filesystem access than the configured ceiling.

Policy sources (monotonic tightening):
- Global: `tools.fs`
- Per-agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- Subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- Sessions-spawn defaults/ceiling: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (applied when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`)
- Spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge semantics:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: enforced as a ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

Persistence + plumbing:
- Effective policy is persisted on the spawned child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) via `sessions.patch`.
- Gateway plumbs it into the child run as ingress field `toolFsPolicy`.
- Gateway session resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve `spawnedToolFsPolicy` (no widening after reset).
- Embedded runner passes it into tool construction.
- `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an `fsPolicy` override and uses it as the effective policy for that run.
- Nested spawns inherit the parent session’s `spawnedToolFsPolicy` ceiling before applying config/tool-call tightening.

---

## Configuration (for contributors)

### Filesystem policy knobs

- `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: boolean`
- `tools.fs.allowedPaths: string[] | undefined` (empty array means deny-all)
- `tools.fs.denyPaths: string[] | undefined`

Subagent ceiling:
- `tools.subagents.fs.{workspaceOnly, allowedPaths, denyPaths}`

Sessions-spawn defaults + spawn-time tightening:
- `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (config default ceiling/tightening)
- `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (tool arg tightening; can only further restrict)

Notes:
- Deny always wins.
- Relative entries are workspace-anchored.
- Policies are evaluated against canonicalized real paths.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals / Out of Scope

- Exec sandboxing is not implemented here.
  R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”
- No shell argument parsing.
- Remote source governance is out-of-scope.

---

## Implementation Notes (for contributors)

### Policy evaluation edge cases covered

- `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit **deny-all** (not “no restriction”).
- Relative literal entries are **re-anchored after realpath**: if a workspace-relative entry canonicalizes outside the workspace (e.g. it points at a symlink to an external dir), it **does not** match outside-workspace targets.
- Absolute glob entries support symlink-alias authoring by **canonicalizing the non-glob prefix** before matching.
  - When rebuilding the rewritten pattern, the implementation **normalizes slash joining** so root-anchored globs stay semantically equivalent (e.g. `"/**/*.pem"` must not become `"//**/*.pem"`).

### Tool behavior on denial (model guidance)

When a filesystem access is blocked by PathGuard, tools return a structured `policy_denied` result (English text + structured fields) so models:
- do not attempt “bypass retries”,
- clearly communicate what was attempted,
- present actionable remediation options,
- and point to config keys (`tools.fs.*`) for operators to change if access is intended.

### Subagent filesystem ceiling (monotonic tightening)

Effective subagent policy is computed as a ceiling and is monotonic (cannot be widened by spawn calls):
- global: `tools.fs`
- agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- sessions-spawn defaults: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`
- spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` / tool arg `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge intent:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

The effective ceiling is persisted on the child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) and forwarded into the embedded runner (`toolFsPolicy` → `createOpenClawCodingTools({ fsPolicy })`).

---

## Verification

- CI checks and tests pass (per latest runs).
- Added/maintained coverage ensuring `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` is applied as a default tightening when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`.
- Verified critical denial scenarios: traversal, symlink/junction escapes, ENOENT parent-walk, deny precedence, empty allowlist deny-all, sandbox media enforcement, apply_patch enforcement, and subagent ceiling propagation.
- Verified monotonic nested spawning: a tightened subagent cannot spawn a child with broader FS access (parent `spawnedToolFsPolicy` is inherited).
- Verified gateway resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve the spawned FS ceiling (no widening after reset).

---

## Reviewer / Contributor Notes (Do not regress these invariants)

- Do not replace per-path enforcement with root-prefix approximations for glob policies.
- Keep deny-overrides-allow.
- Keep relative policy entries anchored to workspace (including after realpath).
- Keep sandbox and host local-file enforcement aligned.
- Keep local filesystem policy and remote-source policy separate concepts.
- Keep subagent policy tightening monotonic (no privilege escalation vs ceiling).
- Remember: exec is outside this enforcement layer.

---

## Rollback / Recovery

- Disable restrictions by setting `workspaceOnly: false` and clearing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths`.
- No data migration required.

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration * Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) * No * Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) * No * If yes, exact upgrade steps: * N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds display-width and grapheme-aware padding plus CJK/emoji regression tests for markdown code-mode tables in the old `src/markdown` renderer.

target.title: Expose verified ClawHub source in skill verify output
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #92077. Follow-up to #93506.

This PR exposes the trusted ClawHub source provenance that #93506 started consuming in one more user-visible surface:

- Adds `openclaw.verifiedSourceUrl` to `openclaw skills verify` JSON output when ClawHub verification returns server-resolved GitHub provenance.
- Reuses the same trust-boundary helper for install persistence and verify output, so unsupported provenance shapes remain untrusted.
- Builds the promoted source URL from server-resolved `repo` + `commit` + optional `path`, producing a commit-pinned GitHub URL instead of trusting the raw `provenance.url` field, which can point at a mutable ref such as `main`.
- Documents the new verify JSON field in the skills CLI docs.

This does not read or promote self-attested `SKILL.md` metadata from #92860. It also does not replace #93314, which is still the readback half for provenance already written to local `.clawhub/origin.json`.

## Behavior addressed

Users and tooling consuming `openclaw skills verify` JSON can now find the verified source URL under the OpenClaw-owned metadata envelope without guessing whether raw ClawHub `provenance.url` is immutable or trusted.

Unavailable provenance or any non-`server-resolved-github-import` provenance stays only in the raw verification envelope and is not promoted as `openclaw.verifiedSourceUrl`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw skills verify` JSON now promotes only server-resolved ClawHub GitHub provenance into a commit-pinned `openclaw.verifiedSourceUrl`.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS with Node v22.18.0 and pnpm 11.2.2. The local Node version is below the repo-requested `>=22.19.0`, so commands print the existing engine warning, but all targeted checks exited 0.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/skills-cli.verify.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.ts src/cli/skills-cli.ts src/cli/skills-cli.verify.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts docs/cli/skills.md
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.ts src/cli/skills-cli.ts src/cli/skills-cli.verify.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm tsgo:test:src
git diff --check
pnpm docs:list
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts: 40 passed
src/cli/skills-cli.verify.test.ts: 3 passed
oxfmt: all matched files use the correct format
oxlint: passed on changed source/test files
tsgo:core: passed
tsgo:test:src: passed
git diff --check: passed
docs:list: passed
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct
```

Observed result after fix:

- A verification response with `provenance.source: "server-resolved-github-import"`, `url: ".../tree/main/agentreceipt"`, `repo: "openclaw/skills"`, `commit: "def456"`, and `path: "agentreceipt"` produces `openclaw.verifiedSourceUrl: "https://github.com/openclaw/skills/tree/def456/agentreceipt"`.
- A verification response with `provenance.source: "unavailable"` and a URL does not produce `openclaw.verifiedSourceUrl`.
- Install persistence continues using the same helper, so server-resolved provenance stored in local lock/origin metadata is also commit-pinned.

What was not tested: there is still no live positive ClawHub provenance sample available from production ClawHub, so this PR uses focused CLI/lifecycle tests around the current server-resolved provenance contract.

target.title: fix/agents-primary-model-selection-and-catalog-loading-41344
target.body: ## Summary - Fixes incorrect Primary Model selection when switching between agents in Agents > Overview. - Fixes partial / blank Primary Model dropdowns after refresh or first load by ensuring the model catalog is available on Agents Overview paths. - Adds targeted tests for both selection stability and catalog hydration behavior. - Keeps the fix scoped to the Agents Overview model-selection path. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #41344 - Related #46275 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: 1. The agent details subtree could reuse stale DOM/select state when switching agents, which caused the Primary Model select to drift toward the previously viewed agent's state. 2. Agents Overview depended on a shared model catalog that was not guaranteed to be loaded on refresh / first-load / overview-only paths, so the select could render before the catalog was ready and degrade into a partial or blank-looking list. - Missing detection / guardrail: - Existing coverage covered some selected-value behavior, but it did not cover model-catalog hydration timing or the refresh/overview path where the catalog starts empty and arrives later. - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - Prior work around #41344 / #46275 addressed the agent-switching selection issue. Revalidating that fix on latest `upstream/main` exposed a second, related lifecycle issue in Agents Overview model-catalog loading. - Why this regressed now: - The Overview UI depends on model-catalog data, but not all entry paths guaranteed that the catalog was loaded before rendering. - If unknown, what was ruled out: - Ruled out a pure selected-state-only explanation. The partial/blank dropdown after refresh was caused by catalog availability/lifecycle, not just DOM selection drift. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.browser.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - Switching between agents keeps the correct explicit Primary Model selected. - The inherit-default option text does not go blank. - When the model catalog is initially empty, Agents Overview shows a loading state instead of a misleading partial list. - When the catalog hydrates later, the full list appears without losing the selected value. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - The bug spans both UI render behavior and shared model-catalog lifecycle, so a combination of focused controller/unit tests plus browser-level select behavior tests is the smallest reliable coverage. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - Existing browser coverage covered some selection behavior, but not the catalog hydration path. - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Switching between agents no longer causes the Primary Model select to show the wrong agent's value. - On refresh / first entry to Agents Overview, the Primary Model select no longer degrades into a misleading partial list. - While the model catalog is still loading, the UI now shows a loading state instead of pretending only a small subset of options exists. - Once the catalog loads, the full option list appears without losing the current selection. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 10 - Runtime/container: local dev environment - Model/provider: local UI validation only - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): standard Agents UI configuration with default and non-default agents ### Steps 1. Open Agents > Overview > Model Selection > Primary Model. 2. Switch between a default agent and a non-default agent. 3. Refresh the page and reopen the Primary Model select immediately. 4. Optionally wait for model-catalog hydration or switch away/back to Overview. ### Expected - The select reflects the correct explicit value for the current agent. - The inherit-default option text is stable. - Refresh / first-load does not show a misleading partial catalog. - Once the catalog is available, the full model list appears without losing the selected value. ### Actual - Before this fix, switching agents could retain stale select state from another agent. - Before this fix, refresh / first-load could show only fallback options (for example inherit/current) or appear blank until the catalog became available later. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ### UI Evidence #### Before After refresh / first load, the Primary Model dropdown could show only fallback entries or appear blank. #### After After the fix, the Primary Model dropdown restores the full option list correctly after refresh / first load, without losing the selected value. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Switched repeatedly between default and non-default agents and confirmed Primary Model stays tied to the current agent. - Refreshed the page and reopened Agents Overview to verify the select no longer falls into a misleading partial/blank state. - Confirmed catalog hydration restores the full list without losing the selected value. - Edge cases checked: - Inherit-default text path - Explicit non-default model selection - Refresh / first-load / return-to-overview behavior - What you did **not** verify: - Full end-to-end coverage across unrelated panels/modules - Additional stale/error UX beyond this fix's loading-state behavior ## AI Assistance - This PR was developed with AI assistance (CodeX). - I reviewed the resulting changes, reproduced the bug locally, and verified the final behavior manually. - Testing level: fully tested locally with targeted automated tests and manual UI verification. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: - N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: - Revert this PR's two commits. - Files/config to restore: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents-panels-overview.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-render.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-settings.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-chat.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: - Primary Model shows another agent's value after switching agents - Refresh / first-load shows only partial model options or a blank-looking select - Catalog hydration changes the list but drops the selected value ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: - Multiple UI entry points now ensure model-catalog availability, which could increase repeated refresh attempts if not properly deduped. - Mitigation: - The loading/ensure logic is centralized in the shared model controller and covered by targeted tests. - Risk: - Keyed subtree remounting can discard unsaved local UI state when switching agents. - Mitigation: - That tradeoff is intentional here because correctness of agent-specific model selection is more important than preserving stale subtree state across agent switches.

target.title: fix(outbound): strip inbound metadata before delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #39847. Related to #41355, #30405, #28170.

When an LLM echoes internal metadata blocks (e.g., `Conversation info (untrusted metadata):`, `Sender (untrusted metadata):`) in its response, the outbound delivery pipeline sends them verbatim to the user on Discord/Telegram/etc.

`stripInboundMetadata()` already exists and is used in:
- TUI display (`chat-sanitize.ts`)
- Session cost calculations (`session-cost-usage.ts`)

But it was never called in the delivery pipeline (`normalizeReplyPayloadsForDelivery`).

## Changes

- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts` — import and call `stripInboundMetadata()` on payload text before parsing reply directives
- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.test.ts` — add test verifying metadata blocks are stripped before delivery

## Test plan

- [x] Added test: payload with echoed metadata block → metadata stripped, actual response preserved
- [x] All 9 tests in `payloads.test.ts` pass
- [x] `pnpm build && pnpm check` — clean

> AI-assisted: test case co-authored with Claude (Anthropic).
